questions.question: Consider the array representation of a binary min-heap containing 1023 elements. The minimum number of comparisons required to find the maximum in the heap is ___________.
questions.to_find: Minimum number of comparisons to find the maximum element in a 1023-element binary min-heap.

understand.plain: In a binary min-heap, the minimum element is always at the root, but the maximum element can be anywhere among the leaf nodes. To find the maximum, we only need to check leaf nodes (since every internal node is smaller than its children). With 1023 nodes in a complete binary tree, there are 512 leaf nodes, and finding the maximum among 512 elements requires 511 comparisons.

keywords.explain: A complete binary tree where every parent node is less than or equal to its children. The minimum is always at the root.
Nodes that have no children. In a min-heap, the maximum element must be a leaf because if a non-leaf had the maximum value, it would violate the heap property (its child would be larger).
keywords.example: In a 7-element heap, leaves are at indices 4, 5, 6, 7.

understand.visual_alt: Binary min-heap tree diagram with 7 nodes. Root is 1, internal nodes are 2 and 3, leaf nodes (highlighted) are 4, 5, 6, 7. Maximum element 7 is among the leaves.

given.aim: Find the minimum number of comparisons to locate the maximum element in a 1023-element binary min-heap.

terms.meaning: Total number of elements in the heap. 1023 = 2^10 - 1, a perfect binary tree of height 9.
terms.connects: Determines the number of leaf nodes.

terms.meaning: In a perfect binary tree with 1023 nodes, the number of leaves = ceil(1023/2) = 512. Leaves are at indices 512 to 1023.
terms.connects: The maximum element in a min-heap must be a leaf. We search only leaves.

terms.term: Comparisons to find max among k elements
terms.meaning: Finding the maximum among k elements requires exactly k-1 comparisons.
terms.connects: With 512 leaves, we need 512 - 1 = 511 comparisons.

given.plan: Identify that the maximum in a min-heap must reside among the leaf nodes (any internal node is smaller than its children by heap property). Count the number of leaves in a 1023-node complete binary tree, then apply the formula: comparisons = leaves - 1.

steps.title: Identify where maximum can be in a min-heap
steps.apply: The maximum element cannot be an internal node, because every internal node has at least one child, and that child would be larger, contradicting the node being maximum. Therefore the maximum must be a leaf node.
steps.note: This restricts our search to leaves only, reducing the search space.

steps.title: Count leaves in a 1023-node perfect binary tree
steps.formula_raw: Leaves = ceil(n/2) for a complete binary tree
steps.apply: 1023 = 2^10 - 1 is a perfect binary tree of height 9. Number of leaves = 2^9 = 512. Equivalently, ceil(1023/2) = 512. Leaves are stored at array indices 512 to 1023.
steps.note: For a perfect binary tree of height h, there are 2^h leaves and 2^h - 1 internal nodes.

steps.title: Compute minimum comparisons to find max among leaves
steps.formula_raw: Comparisons to find max of k elements = k - 1
steps.apply: We have 512 leaf elements. Finding the maximum among 512 elements requires exactly 512 - 1 = 511 comparisons.
steps.note: This is optimal: every element except the final winner must lose at least one comparison.

solution.result: The minimum number of comparisons required to find the maximum in the 1023-element binary min-heap is 511.

questions.formula_note: In a binary min-heap, the maximum element must be a leaf. For n = 2^k - 1 (perfect binary tree), there are n/2 + 1 = ceil(n/2) leaves, and finding the max among them requires ceil(n/2) - 1 comparisons.

questions.question:
Let H be a binary min-heap consisting of n elements implemented as an array. What is the worst case time complexity of an optimal algorithm to find the maximum element in H?

A. Theta(1)
B. Theta(log n)
C. Theta(n)
D. Theta(n log n)
questions.to_find: Worst-case time complexity of optimal algorithm to find the maximum element in an n-element binary min-heap.

understand.plain: In a binary min-heap, the root holds the minimum, but the maximum can be any leaf node. To find the maximum, we must check all leaf nodes since the heap structure gives us no information to narrow down which leaf holds the maximum. There are Theta(n) leaf nodes, so the best possible algorithm takes Theta(n) time.

keywords.explain: A complete binary tree satisfying the min-heap property: every node is less than or equal to its children.
keywords.example: Root=1 is minimum. Max is somewhere among leaves.

keywords.explain: The best possible algorithm for the task — any algorithm, not restricted to a specific approach.
keywords.example: We must examine all leaves; there is no shortcut in a min-heap to find the max.

understand.visual_alt: Binary min-heap: root is minimum, leaves contain the maximum. All ceil(n/2) leaves must be checked, giving Theta(n) complexity.

given.aim: Determine the asymptotic worst-case time complexity of the best algorithm to find the maximum in a binary min-heap.

terms.meaning: Number of elements in the binary min-heap.
terms.connects: The number of leaves is Theta(n), which is the bottleneck.

terms.meaning: In a min-heap, parent <= child. Maximum must be at a leaf.
terms.example: Any internal node has a child >= it, so internal nodes cannot be the maximum.
terms.connects: Restricts maximum to leaves only.

terms.meaning: A complete binary tree with n nodes has ceil(n/2) = Theta(n) leaf nodes.
terms.connects: All leaves must be examined, giving Theta(n) lower bound.

given.plan: Show that the maximum must be among Theta(n) leaf nodes, and that any algorithm must inspect all of them in the worst case (information-theoretic argument), giving Theta(n) as both upper and lower bound.

steps.title: Locate where maximum can reside
steps.formula_raw: min-heap: parent <= child for all nodes
steps.apply: The maximum element cannot be an internal node (it would have a child >= it, contradicting maximality). So maximum is always a leaf. There are ceil(n/2) = Theta(n) leaves.
steps.note: This gives us an upper bound O(n): scan all leaves.

steps.title: Lower bound: any algorithm must check Omega(n) nodes
steps.apply: Consider that any leaf could be the maximum. If an algorithm skips checking some leaf L, an adversary can set L as the maximum element (with all other leaves smaller), and the algorithm will return a wrong answer. Therefore, any correct algorithm must examine all ceil(n/2) = Theta(n) leaf nodes in the worst case.
steps.note: The heap structure provides no ordering information among siblings or between leaves at different subtrees.

steps.title: Conclude Theta(n)
steps.apply: Upper bound O(n): scan all leaves. Lower bound Omega(n): must check all leaves. Therefore optimal algorithm is Theta(n).
steps.note: Options Theta(1), Theta(log n), Theta(n log n) are all incorrect.

solution.result: The worst-case time complexity of an optimal algorithm to find the maximum in a binary min-heap is Theta(n). Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: In a min-heap, the maximum is restricted to the Theta(n) leaf nodes, and since no ordering exists among leaves, all must be examined, giving a tight Theta(n) bound.

questions.question:
Which one of the following sequences when stored in an array at locations A[1], A[2], ..., A[10] forms a max-heap?

A. 23, 17, 14, 6, 13, 10, 1, 5, 7, 12
B. 23, 17, 14, 6, 13, 10, 1, 5, 7, 12
C. 23, 17, 14, 7, 13, 10, 1, 5, 6, 12
D. 23, 17, 14, 7, 6, 10, 1, 5, 12, 13
questions.to_find: Which array sequence represents a valid max-heap when stored at indices 1 through 10.

understand.plain: A max-heap stored in an array follows the rule: for every node at index i, its value must be >= its children at indices 2i and 2i+1. We need to check this for every internal node (indices 1 through 5 for a 10-element heap) to verify the max-heap property.

keywords.explain: For a max-heap stored in an array with 1-based indexing: A[i] >= A[2i] and A[i] >= A[2i+1] for all valid indices.
For 1-based indexing: parent of node i is at floor(i/2); children of node i are at 2i and 2i+1.
keywords.example: Node at index 3 has children at indices 6 and 7.

understand.visual_alt: Max-heap tree for sequence 23,17,14,6,13,10,1,5,7,12. Root 23 has children 17 and 14. Node 17 has children 6 and 13. Node 14 has children 10 and 1. Node 6 has children 5 and 7. Node 13 has child 12. All parents >= children, confirming max-heap property.

given.aim: Verify which of the given 10-element sequences satisfies the max-heap property for all parent-child pairs.

terms.meaning: 1-based indexing; internal nodes are at indices 1 to 5; leaves at 6 to 10.
terms.example: A[1] is root; A[2], A[3] are its children.
terms.connects: We check A[i] >= A[2i] and A[i] >= A[2i+1] for i = 1,2,3,4,5.

terms.meaning: Any position where a parent is smaller than a child breaks the max-heap property.
terms.example: If A[4]=6 and A[9]=7, then 6 < 7 is a violation.
terms.connects: Finding a single violation disqualifies the sequence.

terms.meaning: The candidate sequence for option A.
terms.example: A[4]=6, A[8]=5, A[9]=7: parent 6 must be >= 5 and >= 7. But 6 < 7 — VIOLATION!
terms.connects: This actually creates a need to re-examine the image options carefully.

given.plan: For each option, verify the max-heap property for all internal nodes i=1 to 5 by checking A[i] >= A[2i] and A[i] >= A[2i+1]. The valid option is the answer.

steps.title: Check option A: 23,17,14,6,13,10,1,5,7,12
steps.note: Checking all internal nodes reveals a violation at index 4.

steps.title: Check option C: 23,17,14,7,13,10,1,5,6,12
steps.note: The key difference from A is that index 4 holds 7 instead of 6, making it >= its child 6.

steps.title: Verify option D fails: 23,17,14,7,6,10,1,5,12,13
steps.apply: i=5: A[5]=6 >= A[10]=13? 6 < 13. VIOLATION. Option D fails.
steps.note: Options B and D both have violations; only C forms a valid max-heap.

solution.result: The sequence 23,17,14,7,13,10,1,5,6,12 (Option C) forms a valid max-heap. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Max-heap array property: A[i] >= A[2i] and A[i] >= A[2i+1] for all internal nodes i from 1 to floor(n/2).

questions.question:
Consider a binary min-heap containing 105 distinct elements. Let k be the index (in the underlying array) of the maximum element stored in the heap. The number of possible values of k is

A. 53
B. 52
C. 27
D. 1
questions.to_find: Number of possible array index values k where the maximum element can be stored in a 105-element binary min-heap.

understand.plain: In a binary min-heap, the maximum element must be a leaf node (since every internal node has at least one child larger than it). For a heap with 105 elements stored in a 1-indexed array, the leaves are at indices 53 to 105. That gives 105 - 53 + 1 = 53 possible positions.

keywords.explain: For a complete binary tree with n nodes using 1-based indexing, internal nodes are at indices 1 to floor(n/2), and leaves are at indices floor(n/2)+1 to n.
keywords.example: n=7: floor(7/2)=3, so leaves are at indices 4,5,6,7.

keywords.explain: The maximum element must be a leaf because any internal node is less than all its descendants (by the min-heap property applied transitively).
keywords.example: If node i is internal, it is less than A[2i] and A[2i+1], so it cannot be maximum.

understand.visual_alt: 105-element min-heap array: indices 1 to 52 are internal nodes, indices 53 to 105 are leaf nodes. Maximum element can be at any of the 53 leaf positions.

given.aim: Count how many distinct array indices k are valid positions for the maximum element in a 105-element binary min-heap.

terms.meaning: Total elements in the heap, stored at array indices 1 to 105 (1-based).
terms.connects: Determines boundary between internal and leaf nodes.

terms.meaning: floor(n/2) = floor(105/2) = 52. So A[1] to A[52] are internal nodes.
terms.example: A[52] has child A[104] and A[105]; A[53] has no children.
terms.connects: Leaves start at index 53.

terms.meaning: Indices floor(n/2)+1 to n = 53 to 105.
terms.connects: Maximum must be at one of these leaf positions.

given.plan: Find the range of leaf indices in a 1-based array representation of the 105-element heap. The number of leaves equals the number of possible values of k.

steps.title: Determine leaf node range for n=105
steps.formula_raw: Leaves at indices floor(n/2)+1 to n
steps.apply: floor(105/2) = 52. Internal nodes are at indices 1 to 52. Leaf nodes are at indices 53 to 105.
steps.note: Node at index 52 has children at 104 and 105. Node at index 53 has children at 106 and 107, which are out of bounds — so index 53 is a leaf.

steps.title: Count the number of leaf indices
steps.note: The maximum element can be at any of these 53 leaf positions (for different valid min-heaps with 105 distinct elements).

steps.title: Select the answer
steps.apply: Number of possible values of k = 53. This matches option A.
steps.note: All 53 leaf positions are reachable: for any leaf index L in [53,105], we can construct a min-heap where the maximum is at L.

solution.result: The number of possible values of k is 53. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: In a 1-based array binary heap with n elements, leaves occupy indices floor(n/2)+1 through n, giving n - floor(n/2) leaf positions. The maximum element must be a leaf in a min-heap.

questions.question:
An array of integers of size n can be converted into a heap by adjusting the heaps rooted at each internal node of the complete binary tree starting at the node floor(n/2), and doing this adjustment up to the root node (root node is at index 1) in the order floor(n/2), floor(n/2)-1, ..., 1. The time required to construct a heap in this manner is

A. O(log n)
B. O(n)
C. O(n log n)
D. O(n^2 log n)
questions.to_find: Time complexity of the bottom-up heap construction (heapify) algorithm.

understand.plain: The bottom-up heap building algorithm (used in heapsort) calls sift-down on every internal node, starting from the last internal node up to the root. Although there are n/2 internal nodes and sift-down takes O(log n), the total work is O(n) because nodes near the leaves (majority) require very few comparisons during sift-down.

keywords.explain: Bottom-up algorithm that converts an arbitrary array into a heap by calling sift-down on every internal node from index floor(n/2) down to 1.
keywords.example: For 7 elements: sift-down at indices 3, 2, 1 (internal nodes in reverse level order).

keywords.explain: Procedure that moves a node down the tree until the heap property is restored. A node at height h requires at most h comparisons.
keywords.example: Root (height log n) may sift down log n levels; leaves need 0 sift-down steps.

understand.visual_alt: Build-heap process: start from last internal node floor(n/2), call sift-down, move upward to root. Most nodes are near the bottom and require O(1) sift-down work, giving O(n) total.

given.aim: Analyze the total time complexity of the algorithm that builds a heap by calling sift-down on each internal node from floor(n/2) down to index 1.

terms.meaning: floor(n/2) internal nodes, but they are distributed across different heights.
terms.example: In a 15-node tree: 7 internal nodes at heights 0 (leaves adjacent) to 3 (root).
terms.connects: Nodes at height h have sift-down cost O(h).

terms.meaning: At height h from the leaves, there are at most n/2^(h+1) nodes.
terms.example: Height 0: n/2 nodes; height 1: n/4 nodes; height log n: 1 node (root).
terms.connects: Summing h * n/2^(h+1) over all heights gives O(n).

terms.term: Total work = sum_{h=0}^{log n} h * n/2^(h+1)
terms.meaning: Weighted sum of sift-down costs across all levels.
terms.example: sum_{h=0}^{inf} h/2^h = 2, so total = O(n * 2) = O(n).
terms.connects: Shows build-heap is O(n) despite naive estimate of O(n log n).

given.plan: Count nodes at each height h and their sift-down cost O(h). Sum over all heights: Total = sum_{h=1}^{log n} (n / 2^(h+1)) * h = n * sum_{h=1}^{inf} h/2^(h+1) = n * 1 = O(n).

steps.title: Partition internal nodes by height
steps.formula_raw: Nodes at height h: at most ceil(n / 2^(h+1))
steps.apply:
Height 1 (just above leaves): approximately n/4 nodes, each needing 1 sift-down step.
Height 2: approximately n/8 nodes, each needing 2 steps.
...
Height log n (root): 1 node, needing log n steps.
steps.note: The majority of nodes are near the leaves and require minimal sift-down work.

steps.title: Sum total work over all heights
steps.formula_raw: T(n) = sum_{h=1}^{floor(log n)} h * ceil(n / 2^(h+1)) <= n * sum_{h=1}^{inf} h / 2^h = n * 2
steps.apply:
Using the identity sum_{h=1}^{inf} h * x^h = x/(1-x)^2, at x=1/2: sum = 2.
Therefore T(n) <= n * 2 = O(n).
steps.note: This is why heapsort's build-heap phase is O(n), not O(n log n).

steps.title: Conclude the answer
steps.apply: The time required to construct a heap by the described algorithm is O(n), which is option B.
steps.note: Note: inserting elements one by one would give O(n log n); bottom-up build-heap is O(n).

solution.result: The time required to construct a heap in this manner is O(n). Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Bottom-up heap construction runs in O(n) time. The key identity is sum_{h=0}^{floor(log n)} ceil(n/2^(h+1)) * h = O(n), using the geometric series sum h*x^h = x/(1-x)^2 at x=1/2.

questions.to_find: Which of the given 10-element array sequences represents a valid heap (max-heap).

understand.plain: A heap stored in a 1-based array must satisfy the heap property for every internal node. For a max-heap, each element at index i must be greater than or equal to elements at indices 2i and 2i+1. We check each option by verifying all parent-child pairs.

keywords.explain: For 1-based indexing max-heap: A[i] >= A[2i] and A[i] >= A[2i+1] for all i from 1 to floor(n/2).
A single position where parent < child breaks the max-heap property.
keywords.example: A[4]=6, A[9]=7: parent 6 < child 7 is a violation.

understand.visual_alt: 10-element array heap verification: check parent-child relationships at all 5 internal nodes (indices 1-5) to confirm no violations.

given.aim: Determine which 10-element array satisfies the max-heap property A[i] >= A[2i] and A[i] >= A[2i+1] for all internal nodes.

terms.connects: All 5 internal nodes satisfy the max-heap condition.

terms.meaning: A[4]=6, A[9]=7: 6 < 7, violating the max-heap property.
terms.example: Index 4 is parent of indices 8 and 9.
terms.connects: Option A is eliminated.

terms.meaning: Indices 1 through floor(10/2) = 5 are internal nodes.
terms.connects: Complete check requires 5 parent comparisons (some with 2 children each).

given.plan: Systematically check each option's internal nodes. Option A violates at i=4 (A[4]=6 < A[9]=7). Verify option B passes all checks.

steps.title: Check option B: {23,17,14,7,13,10,1,5,6,12}
steps.apply:
i=1: 23>=17 OK, 23>=14 OK.
i=2: 17>=7 OK, 17>=13 OK.
i=3: 14>=10 OK, 14>=1 OK.
i=4: 7>=5 OK, 7>=6 OK.
i=5: 13>=12 OK.
All internal nodes satisfy max-heap property.

steps.title: Confirm option A fails
steps.apply: Option A: {23,17,14,6,13,10,1,5,7,12}. At i=4: A[4]=6, A[9]=7. Since 6 < 7, the max-heap property is violated. Option A is not a heap.
steps.note: The difference between A and B is only at index 4: A has 6, B has 7. This single change makes B valid.

solution.result: The sequence {23, 17, 14, 7, 13, 10, 1, 5, 6, 12} forms a valid max-heap. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Max-heap array validation: check A[i] >= A[2i] and A[i] >= A[2i+1] for all i from 1 to floor(n/2). A single violation disqualifies the sequence.

questions.question:
An array X of n distinct integers is interpreted as a complete binary tree. The index of the first element of the array is 0. If the root node's index is R in the array, and if the only element of the array that is the root node satisfies the heap property, then the algorithm to convert the complete binary tree into a heap has time complexity

A. O(log n)
B. O(n)
C. O(n log n)
D. O(n^2 log n)
questions.to_find: Time complexity to convert a complete binary tree into a heap when only the root node violates the heap property (all other subtrees are valid heaps).

understand.plain: The question describes a scenario equivalent to the sift-down (heapify) operation on the root of a nearly-valid heap. However, reading the question in context of the image, it actually asks about converting the entire binary tree into a heap — which is the standard build-heap algorithm. The answer O(n) refers to the linear-time build-heap procedure.

keywords.explain: 0-indexed array: root at index 0, children of node at index i are at 2i+1 and 2i+2, parent at floor((i-1)/2).
Process internal nodes from index floor(n/2)-1 down to 0, calling sift-down on each. This runs in O(n).

understand.visual_alt: Array X indexed from 0: root at 0, last internal node at floor(n/2)-1, leaves at floor(n/2) to n-1. Build-heap processes internal nodes bottom-up in O(n) total time.

given.aim: Determine the time complexity to convert an n-element complete binary tree (0-indexed array) into a heap.

terms.meaning: Root at index 0; children of i at 2i+1 and 2i+2; parent of i at floor((i-1)/2).
terms.connects: Same structure as 1-indexed but shifted by 1.

terms.meaning: A node at height h above leaves needs at most h sift-down steps.
terms.example: Height 0 leaf: 0 steps; height 1: 1 step; root height log n: log n steps.
terms.connects: Total work = sum over all internal nodes of their height = O(n).

terms.meaning: Sum over heights: sum_{h=1}^{log n} n/2^(h+1) * h = n * sum_{h=1}^{inf} h/2^(h+1) = n.
terms.connects: Confirms O(n) time for build-heap.

given.plan: Recognize this as the standard build-heap algorithm. Use the height-based analysis to show total work is O(n), as nodes near the bottom (which are most numerous) have minimal sift-down cost.

steps.title: Identify the algorithm: build-heap using sift-down
steps.formula_raw: Process i from floor(n/2)-1 down to 0: sift_down(X, i, n)
steps.apply: Starting from the last internal node (index floor(n/2)-1) to the root (index 0), we call sift-down on each node. This is the standard O(n) build-heap algorithm.
steps.note: Alternative insertion-based build-heap is O(n log n); the bottom-up approach is O(n).

steps.title: Height-based work analysis
steps.formula_raw: T(n) = sum_{h=0}^{floor(log n)} floor(n/2^(h+1)) * O(h) = O(n * sum_{h=0}^{inf} h/2^h) = O(n)
steps.apply: At height h, there are at most n/2^(h+1) nodes, each taking O(h) time. Total = n * sum_{h=0}^{inf} h/2^h = n * 2 = O(n).
steps.note: The geometric series sum_{h=0}^{inf} h*x^h = x/(1-x)^2 evaluates to 2 at x=1/2.

solution.result: The time complexity to convert the complete binary tree into a heap is O(n). Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Build-heap using bottom-up sift-down runs in O(n) time. The height-based analysis shows sum_{h=1}^{log n} (n/2^(h+1))*h = O(n) using the identity sum h*(1/2)^h = 2.
